entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Sixth Archetype the Maps Forgot
entry.content:
The Sixth Archetype the Maps Forgot

Post 0, The Agentic AI Team series

There was a time when photography was a planned profession. Film gave you thirty-six frames, each one cost money, and none of them existed until a lab developed the roll days later. So the work happened before the shutter. You scouted the location, waited for the light, composed the frame in your head, and spent the shot only when the picture was already made. Scarcity forced planning, and planning was the craft.

Digital removed the scarcity. A frame costs nothing now, so you do not plan the one shot; you take a thousand and throw away nine hundred. The craft did not disappear. It moved. It went from planning the picture to choosing among the pictures, from the setup to the edit. The photographer with an eye still gets the keeper. The amateur with the same camera gets a thousand mediocre frames and no way to tell which one is good.

Product work is having its film-to-digital moment, and the people closest to it have named the new skill well. This series is about the part they left out, and this opening post is about where they left it.

The craft moved to the edit

Boris Cherny, who built Claude Code, posted a small framework that traveled fast. As engineering, product, design, and data science melt together, he argues, job titles stop meaning much, and what is left is five archetypes: the Prototyper who churns ideas, the Builder who makes them production-grade, the Sweeper who simplifies and unships, the Grower who pushes toward fit, the Maintainer who keeps a mature system alive. They cut across function, not along it.

Others gave the underlying skill a name: taste at speed. Evaluate working software fast, kill most of it, ship the survivors. It is a filtering function, not an acceleration function. The high kill rate is the point. Without taste, speed just means building the wrong thing faster. And the line that holds the whole thing together came in a sharp reply from another writer in the thread: agents add, they do not subtract. A model will generate a hundred versions, but knowing which one should exist is judgment it does not have. Delegate the toil, keep the taste.

All of this is right, and it is the photography shift exactly. Building got cheap, so the craft moved from planning the shot to choosing the frame. The lineage is older than the AI framing. Cringely split teams into commandos, infantry, and police in 1992; Wardley into pioneers, settlers, and town planners; Thiel into zero-to-one and one-to-n. What is new is only that lifecycle phase, once the secondary axis, became the primary one. When the camera is free, the eye is the whole job.

The culling itself is not new either, only its position. The industry spent twenty years learning that you cannot plan your way to a good product; you find it by throwing most attempts away. Lean Startup made a virtue of it. A/B testing industrialized it, shipping forty versions of a button to live traffic and keeping the one that won. But all of that culling happened after the thing shipped, paid for in engineering hours and read off a dashboard as user data. The cheap prototype changed the cost and the position: the cull moved upstream, off the live product and onto the contact sheet. You no longer ship an MVP to a thousand users to learn it was the wrong idea. The agent generates a hundred versions on Tuesday, and taste kills ninety-nine by Wednesday, before a user sees one.

I have no argument with any of it. I want to point at the thing it leaves out, because the same people who named the new skill put it at the wrong end of the pipeline.

You do not ship the photo. You ship the photographer.

Every archetype in that list acts on software up to the moment it ships. The Prototyper explores, the Builder hardens, the Sweeper trims, and taste at speed is the judgment that picks the frame before the client ever sees the contact sheet. It is all build-time work. It ends at the shutter.

A photograph is inert. Once you choose the keeper, it is finished; it will never take another picture. This is the point where an agentic product stops behaving like a photograph. You do not ship the photo. You ship the photographer.

An agent keeps deciding after you release it. It shoots at weddings you are not attending, composes in the room, in real time, on a day no one can re-run. Shipping it is not choosing the best of a thousand frames. It is hiring an eye you will not be standing behind, and then living with the pictures it takes when you are not there. Taste at speed cannot reach that moment, because there is no prototype to kill. There is a live agent making the call, and a client who still holds you responsible for it.

This is why the printing-press comparison only takes you halfway. A printing press is a tool. It prints what you set and nothing else. A photographer is an agent; they exercise judgment when you are not there. The press can carry the idea that building got cheap. Only the photographer can carry the idea that you delegated the taste.

The sixth archetype

So there is a sixth archetype, and it is not on the list.

Taste at speed is the photographer culling the shoot before the client sees it. The sixth archetype is the photo editor who reviews that photographer's work across every future assignment, not once at hiring, but shoot after shoot, because now someone else's eye is choosing the keeper and the consequences are still yours. Call it the Supervisor. Its job is not to build the agent or to pick the best version of it before launch. Its job is to watch the released agent decide, catch the assignment where it framed the shot wrong, and stop the picture before it goes to print. Swap the wedding for a loan decision, a triage queue, a procurement approval, a non-refundable booking, and the shot prints the instant the agent takes it, with no editor in the room.

Notice what is strange about this archetype. It has no thirty-year lineage. Cringely did not need it, Wardley did not need it, Thiel did not need it, because in every prior version of the pipeline the thing you shipped held still. The Supervisor exists only when a teammate is probabilistic, when the product keeps making judgment calls after you let go of it. You cannot find it in the old frameworks because the old frameworks never had a member of the team who was not a person.

The closest the old world came was the A/B test, and the gap is the whole point. An A/B test watched the shipped product too, but it watched a number, click-through, conversion, retention, and culled by data anyone could read off a dashboard. The Supervisor watches a judgment, the call the agent just made in a room no dashboard was pointed at. You can automate watching a number. Watching a judgment is the taste the discourse just moved to build time, needed again at run time, where it is harder to get and no one has staffed it.

Why the map leaves it out

There is a reason the most-shared future-of-roles posts describe everything around this seat and never name it. The people drawing the maps are builders describing build work. Their world is prototypes and pull requests and the judgment exercised before the ship gate, and from inside that world the supervision of a running agent is not a role, it is an afterthought that belongs to someone else.

Supervision has never fit the categories we already have. A new hire has HR. A contractor has procurement. A vendor has vendor management. A running agent making unattended decisions fits none of them, so it falls to whoever built it, which means the builder is grading their own homework at the exact moment the stakes go live. The seat that should hold it has not been drawn on the org chart yet. That is not negligence. It is a category the industry has not built, showing up as a blank space in the map everyone is sharing.

Function melts at build, and hardens at run

The strongest claim in the discourse is that function dissolves entirely, that the engineer and the PM and the designer collapse into one person directing agents, and that soon everyone is a builder. Half of that is true, and it is the half you can see.

At build time, function does melt. One fluent person can run all five archetypes, take the idea, prototype it, harden it, trim it, because the agent absorbs the toil inside each one. That is real, and it is happening.

At run time, function hardens again, and it hardens for a reason the build-time view cannot see. The whole value of the Supervisor is that they are not the builder. A photographer cannot be their own editor at the wedding they are shooting; the eye that made the picture is the last one that can judge it clean. The moment you need someone to catch the agent's bad call in production, you need someone whose judgment is independent of the person who shipped it, and independence is a role boundary, the oldest one there is. The agentic team is not post-role. It is role-melted on the channel that builds the agent, and role-split on the channel that watches it. Anyone who has worked near a regulated bank knows the shape: the people who validate the models do not report to the desk that runs them, because supervision that answers to the thing it supervises is not supervision.

The keeper is not the last decision anymore

Film taught a generation of photographers that the decisive moment was the shutter. Digital moved it to the edit. The archetype discourse is the record of product people learning the same lesson: when building is free, the work is choosing.

But an agent moves the decisive moment one step further than the edit, past the ship gate, into every unattended call it makes on your behalf after you have stopped looking. The keeper is no longer the last decision you make. It is the first of many the agent will make without you. Someone has to be the eye on that work, shoot after shoot, and it cannot be the person who took the picture.

That seat is the whole subject of this series, which asks one question across every post: who owns what when the software acts on its own. The five archetypes describe the team that builds the agent. The sixth describes the team that survives it, and the rest of this series is about who sits in it.

This post opens The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which asks who owns what when the software decides and acts on its own. The full framework, including the run-time supervision seat the role maps leave out and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: The Agentic AI Team - The Team Didn't Shrink. It Reshaped.
entry.content:
The Team Didn't Shrink. It Reshaped.

Post 1, The Agentic AI Team series

A team ships an agent that handles procurement. It reads a purchase requisition, checks it against policy, compares suppliers, and places the order, with no human in the middle for the ordinary case. It works. For about six weeks it is the most popular thing the team has built, and then it places a large order against a supplier contract that was terminated the day before, because the termination lived in a system the agent could not see, and nobody on the team can say whose job it had been to notice that the agent could not see it.

Not the engineer, who built exactly what the spec described. Not the product manager, who defined a clear and reasonable goal. Not the designer, who made a clean confirmation screen for a confirmation that, in this case, no human ever saw. The failure did not belong to any of them. Which is another way of saying it belonged to the shape of the team itself.

That shape is the subject of this series, and of the book behind it. The short version is that the team did not shrink when agents arrived. It reshaped, and most teams have not noticed the new shape yet, because the old one still looks like it is working right up until the morning it is not.

The three-seat team was built for the screen

For a generation, a software team had three seats. A product manager who decided what to build, a designer who shaped how it looked and felt, and an engineer who built it. The names and rituals varied, but the shape held across companies and decades because it fit the thing being built so well that nobody thought of it as a choice. It was just what a software team was.

It fit because the thing being built was a screen, a surface a person operates, and the three seats map cleanly onto a screen. Someone decides what the screen should do, someone designs how a person moves through it, someone builds it so it works. The triad was not arbitrary. It was the correct operating model for software that a human drives.

An agentic product is not a screen. It decides and acts on its own, in situations no one specified, after it leaves your hands. Look at the procurement failure again and notice that none of the three seats had a chair for what went wrong. The agent acted without a human approving the specific action. It acted on a picture of the world that was missing a fact. And when it was wrong, there was no path to catch it in the hour it would have been catchable. Those are not bugs in anyone's work. They are responsibilities the three-seat team was never built to hold.

When the system decides, the work moves downstream

For thirty years, the thing an engineer built did what it was told. You wrote the rules, the software followed them, and if it did something you did not expect, that was a bug, a place where the rules you wrote did not match the rules you meant. The whole craft rested on an assumption so deep nobody stated it: the system does what it is instructed, and the work is getting the instructions right.

An agent does not run your rules. It reasons toward a goal you give it and decides the steps itself. You tell it to resolve the requisition, and it decides which supplier, which contract, whether to escalate, whether to ask. Which of those it does is not in any rule you wrote. It chose.

This moves the work. When the system did what it was told, the team's job was upstream, in the deciding and the specifying and the building, and getting the instructions right got the behavior right. When the system decides for itself, the instructions no longer determine the behavior. They bound it and shape it, but the agent will meet situations no one specified and choose anyway. The behavior is now something that happens at runtime, in the world, in cases the team never saw. A whole category of the team's responsibility moved downstream with it, into watching what the thing does. There was no one whose job that was, because until recently there was no such job.

And the watching never ends, because the agent does not hold still. The model underneath it gets updated by a vendor on a Saturday. The data it reads goes stale. The world it operates in shifts, and its behavior drifts with all of them. The agent you launch is not the agent you will have in six months. The team is not building a thing and walking away. It is taking responsibility for a thing that keeps changing after it ships.

Every agentic product is two products

Here is the idea the whole series turns on. Every agentic product is two products.

The first is the agent itself, the thing that decides and acts, the part everyone means when they say they are building an agent. The second is the layer that supervises it: how a human sees what the agent is doing, intervenes before it does something irreversible, investigates when something goes wrong, and stays accountable for the result. Call them Channel 1 and Channel 2. Channel 1 is the agent. Channel 2 is the supervisory layer. They are both products. They both have to be designed, built, and staffed.

Every team knows how to build Channel 1. Channel 2 is the one they forget, and the reason is structural, not a failure of attention. Channel 1 is what the demo shows. It is the impressive part, the part that gets the budget approved. Channel 2 is invisible when everything works, which is most of the time, so it looks like overhead right up until the morning it is the only thing standing between you and the incident. You can build a flawless Channel 1 and ship a liability, and many teams have, because the thing they did not build does not announce its absence until it is too late to build it calmly.

There is a deeper current under this, and it is where the value is going. When the capability becomes a commodity, when any team can wire up a competent agent from the same models everyone else uses, the agent stops being the thing that differentiates the product. What differentiates it is whether you can trust it, and trust is built in Channel 2. The value moves to the layer that supervises.

The line on the ladder

Not every agent needs a heavy Channel 2, and the way to tell is a ladder. An agent holds some amount of authority on a five-rung scale: it suggests, or it drafts, or it acts with approval, or it acts with oversight, or it acts autonomously. The rungs matter less than one line that runs between two of them. It falls between acting with approval and acting with oversight, between a human authorizing each action before it happens and a human seeing actions after they have already happened.

Below the line, every consequential act passes through a person first, so an error-check sits in the path by default and nothing irreversible occurs without someone saying yes to that specific thing. Above the line, the act occurs and the human finds out, a second later or a week later, which means the error-check is gone and has to be rebuilt on purpose somewhere else, or it simply is not there. The procurement agent was above the line, acting on its own with a supervisor watching nothing in particular. Find your own product on the ladder and ask the only question that matters: is it above the line or below it. If it is above, the rest of this series is about the half you have probably not built.

What this means for your team

Channel 2 is not one person's job, and that is the whole point. It comes down to four surfaces the agent touches at the moment it acts: the autonomy boundary, the line between what it may do alone and what it must hand back; the approval moment, where a consequential action pauses for a person and the question of what that person is shown; the audit surface, the durable record of what the agent did and why; and the recovery workflow, the path back when it has done something wrong. Every agentic product has all four whether or not anyone designed them. If you do not decide where the boundary sits, it sits wherever the code happened to put it. If you do not design the recovery, the recovery is whatever the on-call engineer improvises at two in the morning.

None of these belongs cleanly to the PM, the designer, or the engineer. The boundary is a product decision the architect must then physically enforce. The approval moment is a design problem and a domain-judgment problem at once. The audit surface is built by the platform and specified by whoever will need it in an audit. The recovery workflow is owned by whoever runs the thing in production. Some of the owners have names the triad never had: the architect who decides what gets physically enforced rather than merely requested, the eval owner who can say whether the agent is still correct and not just still running, the AgentOps supervisor whose actual job is to watch the running fleet, and the domain expert who knows what a wrong answer costs and who pays it.

On most teams, the honest answer to "who owns this one" is, for at least two of those surfaces, no one. That is the empty half of every agentic product: a second column of a two-column problem, staffed with a team built for one column.

The claim here is not the loud one, that the triad is dead. The roles are not going away, and the chapters ahead are largely about what each of them is becoming, which is something more demanding, not less. The claim is narrower and harder. The triad is no longer sufficient. It is still the right model for Channel 1, the agent a human drives and a screen exposes. What it has no chairs for is Channel 2, the supervisory layer the agentic product drags in behind it. A team that brings only the triad to an agentic product has not brought the wrong team. It has brought half of one.

So the question to carry into the rest of this series is not whether your agent works. The procurement agent worked. The question is who on your team owns the running agent after it ships, and whether that name is real or assumed. If you cannot say it out loud, you have found the empty column. The posts ahead go looking for who belongs in it, one reshaped role at a time.

This post is part of The Agentic AI Team series on SAP Community, adapted from the book "The Agentic AI Team," which examines how the software team reshapes around runtime ownership when the product decides and acts on its own. The full framework, including the role-by-role reshaping and the seams where agentic products actually fail, is at agenticaiproductmanagement.com.

entry.title: AI-Assisted Sales Order Processing with Joule: Less Searching, More Selling
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is becoming an increasingly important part of daily sales operations. With Joule, SAP’s AI copilot, sales representatives can access information faster and reduce administrative tasks. Especially in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Joule helps make sales processes more efficient.

The Challenge

Sales teams often spend significant time:

  • Searching for open customer orders
  • Analyzing sales documents
  • Switching between multiple applications
  • Identifying orders that require attention

Particularly in high-volume sales environments, these activities can have a considerable impact on the efficiency of the sales back office.

How Does Joule Help?

Instead of manually navigating through applications and reports, users can interact with Joule using natural language. For example, they can retrieve open sales orders, filter orders based on specific criteria, or quickly access relevant customer information.

As a result, sales teams benefit from:

  • Faster access to business information
  • Reduced manual search and navigation efforts
  • A more intuitive user experience
  • Faster response times to customers

Another key advantage is support for analyzing sales documents. Open or critical orders, as well as potential fulfillment issues, can be identified more quickly, enabling proactive issue resolution.

Conclusion

Joule demonstrates how generative AI can support modern sales organizations. By providing natural language access to business information and reducing administrative effort, Joule allows sales teams to focus on what matters most: serving customers, identifying opportunities, and driving revenue growth.

Further Information

  • Introducing Joule
  • Joule for Sales | SAP Help Portal
  • Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Flow Builder Plus: Resolving Performance Issues
entry.content:
Introduction

Flow Builder Plus (FCLM_FLOW_BUILDER_2) generates cash flow data for Cash Position and Liquidity Analysis in SAP S/4HANA Finance. Performance issues come up frequently in production, and most trace back to the same few root causes.

If you are new to it, start with SAP Note 3111810- the Technical Setup Guide for Flow Builder Plus. It is worth reading before diving into any troubleshooting.

Running Types at a Glance

Flow Builder Plus has seven running types. Knowing which one is in use is the first thing to check when a performance issue comes in.

#

Running Type

Purpose

1

Cash Position: Delta Run from Staging

Incremental update for cash position

2

Cash Position: Mass Run for Financial Accounting

One-time / rebuild run for FI

3

Delta Run from Staging for Material Management

Incremental update for MM

4

Liquidity Analysis: Delta Run from Staging

Incremental update for liquidity

5

Liquidity Analysis: Mass Run for Financial Accounting

One-time / rebuild run for FI

6

Mass Run for Purchase Ord./Sch. Agreement

One-time / rebuild run for PO/SA

7

Mass Run for Purchase Requisition

One-time / rebuild run for PR

 Mass Runs (rows 2, 5, 6, 7) are one-off executions for migration or data rebuilds. Do not schedule them as regular jobs. For routine updates, use the Delta Run types.

Initial Diagnosis Checklist

When a performance issue is reported, work through these six checks first — they cover the vast majority of cases.

1.  Mass Run or Delta Run?

Check the Running Type in the job variant. Mass Runs process large volumes by design and take longer. Delta Runs should be fast and incremental. Mixing them up leads to the wrong conclusions.

2.  Is the variant correctly configured?

Make sure Parallel Run is enabled with a valid Server Group — without one, the job runs single-threaded regardless of the setting. Also verify Start Items: Mass Runs need 'Input Items', Delta Runs need 'Relevant Items'. The wrong setting pulls in far more data than needed.

3.  Are the relevant performance SAP Notes implemented?

Many reported cases are already fixed in an existing note. Check the Version-Specific SAP Notes section and apply anything missing for your S4CORE version before going further.

4.  Is the job event-based?

Flow Builder Plus works best when triggered by workflow events, so it only runs when new data actually exists. A periodic timer job that fires regardless wastes resources.

For cash position and MM flows, configure event linkage in transaction SWETYPV (see SAP Note 3193587).

5.  Are there uncleared entries in FCLM_FB2_STRTIM?

When a job is manually terminated, temporary tables may not get cleaned up. Check FCLM_FB2_STRTIM for open entries. If any exist:

  • Cancel all active jobs and stop FCLM_FLOWBUILDER_JOB (see SAP Note 2849364).
  • Use transaction FCLM_FB2_UTIL to list all Run IDs and clear the temporary tables by passing the relevant Run ID.

6.  How many entries are in FCLM_FI_STAGING?

Check the record count in FCLM_FI_STAGING. Make sure all existing entries are fully processed before scheduling a new job, a large backlog will significantly slow down the next delta run.

To recover failed entries (XFLAG = X), run program FCLM_FB2_RECOVERY. It can run alongside a live job - no need to stop anything.

What Is Happening: FI Clearing Chain Depth

When using running type Liquidity Analysis: Delta Run from Staging, Flow Builder Plus navigates up to 9 chain steps through FI clearing documents. For every entry in the staging table, it traverses the clearing chain, finds all linked documents relevant for cash flow, and regenerates flows for all of them. The more complex your FI clearing structure, the more documents get processed per run.

This does not surface in Mass Runs because you manually pick the starting document, keeping the scope contained. In Delta Run mode, any FI document can land in the staging table, so the chain traversal can get very wide very quickly.

Last-Resort BAdI Options for Chain Depth (SAP Note 3459738)

First, make sure all performance notes for your version are implemented and Parallel Run is properly configured. That fixes most cases.

If those steps are not enough, three last-resort options are available - all documented in SAP Note 3459738, Symptom 1 -Solutions 2 to 4. All require custom development and should be evaluated thoroughly before going to production.

Option 1: Add a secondary index on FCLM_FB2_FLOW

Table FCLM_FB2_FLOW stores intermediate flows during chain tracing. Adding a secondary Standard index (not Unique, not buffer-only) on fields MANDT, RUNID, STEP, FORWARD_TYPE, OFF_BUKRS, OFF_GJAHR, OFF_BELNR, OFF_BUZEI improves the selections and joins that happen during chain traversal.

Option 2: Reduce chain steps via BAdI

Implement BAdI interface IF_FCLM_FB2_EXT~ADJUST_CHAIN_STEP and change the return value to reduce the chain step from 9 to a lower number. Dropping to 4 (the Flow Builder 1.0 default) gives results comparable to Flow Builder 1.0 with a noticeable improvement in execution time. See SAP Note 3214882 for reference.

Option 3: Cut long chains via BAdI

Implement BAdI interface IF_FCLM_FB2_EXT~FINAL_DECISION_EXT to stop chain traversal at specific documents. Set field FINAL = X in the internal table for the items where Flow Builder Plus should stop. This is useful when you know which document types do not need further chain navigation in your business scenario.

⚠  These are last-resort options. Options 2 and 3 involve custom BAdI implementation; Option 1 adds a database index in the customer namespace. Test thoroughly in a non-production system before applying in production.

Flow Builder Plus: TSV_TNEW_PAGE_ALLOC_FAIL Runtime Error

If Flow Builder Plus jobs are being cancelled with a TSV_TNEW_PAGE_ALLOC_FAIL runtime error, this is covered in detail in:

KBA  3777550  - Flow Builder Plus Job cancelled with runtime error TSV_TNEW_PAGE_ALLOC_FAIL in S/4HANA Cash Management

Version-Specific SAP Notes

Before raising a case with SAP for any Flow Builder Plus issue, implement all notes in the consulting note for your S4CORE version.

Consulting Notes by Version

  • S4CORE 106 (OP 2021): SAP Note 3144000 - SAP S/4HANA Finance, on-premise Edition 2021: Troubleshooting Note for Flow Builder Plus
  • S4CORE 107 (OP 2022): SAP Note 3389195 - SAP S/4HANA Finance, on-premise Edition 2022: Troubleshooting Note for Flow Builder Plus
  • S4CORE 108 (OP 2023): SAP Note 3459738 - SAP S/4HANA Finance, on-premise Edition 2023: Troubleshooting Note for Flow Builder Plus

Key Performance and Fix Notes

SAP Note Description S4CORE Version(s)

3237591Flow Builder Plus: Slow Performance in Packaging106, 107
3286284Flow Builder Plus: Expensive statements of getting start items and packaging106, 107
3296285Flow Builder Plus: Condense logic refinement 106, 107
3300852Flow Builder Plus: Slow Performance in Packaging, Part II106, 107
3311844Flow Builder Plus: Cumulative fixing and improvements 107
3338726Flow Builder Plus: Expensive statements on steps of packaging and finalizing flows 106, 107
3348914Flow Builder Plus: Clearing on GR/IR account is not traced even with BAdI implementation (cannot work after 3300852)106, 107
3360442Flow Builder Plus: Enhancement of the Delta Run Process and Bug Fix in Log Process 106, 107, 108
3390990Flow Builder Plus: Cumulative fixing and improvements 2106, 107
3511762Flow Builder Plus: Performance Improvement of Class CL_FCLM_FLOWBUILDER_2_FIOFFCLR 108
3573953Flow Builder Plus: Performance Improvements 108

Before You Raise a Case with SAP: Quick Checklist

Run through this list in order before opening a support ticket:

  1. Confirm the running type: Mass Run or Delta Run. Different expectations apply to each.
  2. Implement all performance notes for your S4CORE version (see the table above).
  3. Check the variant: Is Parallel Run enabled? Is a valid Server Group set? Are Start Items configured correctly?
  4. Check FCLM_FB2_STRTIM for uncleared temporary entries and clear them via FCLM_FB2_UTIL.
  5. Check FCLM_FI_STAGING record count and use FCLM_FB2_RECOVERY for any failed entries.
  6. If nothing has resolved it: explore the last-resort BAdI options to control chain depth (SAP Note 3459738, Solutions 2 to 4).
  7. Be careful with SAP Note 3467640 if your data volumes are high - it can make things worse, not better.

entry.title: AI in Sales: What Can AI-Based Sales Order Entry Deliver Today?
entry.content:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly becoming part of modern sales operations. One area where it offers significant value is sales order processing, helping organizations reduce manual effort and improve process efficiency.

Many inside sales teams spend a considerable amount of time handling incoming customer orders. Manual data entry, incomplete order information, and the need for customer clarification often lead to delays and errors.

This is where AI can make a difference. Modern solutions analyze existing master and transactional data and support users in the creation of sales orders. For example, missing information can be completed automatically, materials can be suggested, and inconsistencies can be identified at an early stage.

Some of the key benefits include:

  • Faster processing of customer orders
  • Reduced manual data entry
  • Improved data quality
  • Relief from repetitive administrative tasks
  • Higher customer satisfaction through faster response times

As a result, AI in sales is no longer a vision of the future. It is already an important building block for more efficient order-to-cash processes. Companies can streamline operations, improve process quality, and enhance the overall customer experience.

SAP provides a solution called "Create Sales Orders – Automatic Extraction", which leverages SAP Document AI to automatically extract information from customer purchase orders (for example PDFs or image files) and generate proposals for creating sales orders. This is a practical example of how AI can support sales order entry and help organizations increase efficiency in their sales processes.

Further Information: SAP Help Portal – Create Sales Orders – Automatic Extraction

entry.title: Asset Management in SAP: What Sales and Presales Need to Know Before Walking Into the Room
entry.content:
Honest question: when did a customer last bring up equipment uptime, MTTR, or asset maintenance costs — and you felt completely confident navigating that conversation?

If there was even a moment of hesitation, this post is exactly about that.

The Gap Nobody Talks About

Most sales and presales people can walk through plan-to-fulfill, procure-to-pay, or order-to-cash in their sleep. But the moment a COO or plant manager starts asking how SAP handles predictive maintenance, field service dispatch, or sustainability reporting from asset operations — the conversation moves into unfamiliar territory.

That's precisely the gap that the learning journey "Positioning Solutions for Operating in SAP SCM Design to Operate Approach" on SAP Learning is built to close.

Why Operate Is Not Just "Maintenance Stuff"

Assets in oil & gas, utilities, mining, and chemicals run for decades. Every percentage point of uptime, every shortened maintenance cycle, every prevented failure has a direct line to the bottom line. These are not IT decisions. They are operational survival decisions made by COOs, plant managers, and reliability engineers — people who have seen SAP pitches before.

Holding your own in that conversation means knowing their language: MTTR, OEE, condition-based maintenance, MRO optimization. Without it, the conversation either stalls or drifts toward competitors who are genuinely at home in this territory. 

What This Course Actually Gives You

You stop selling modules and start solving problems

Nobody wakes up wanting to buy "SAP S/4HANA Asset Management." People wake up worried about an aging compressor fleet, a workforce retiring faster than it can be replaced, and a CFO asking why maintenance costs keep climbing. The course teaches you to enter the conversation from the customer's reality — their KPIs, their risks, their priorities — and work back to the solution from there. That's a different skill from knowing the feature list, and it's the one that actually moves deals.

You understand the full Operate portfolio — not as a catalog, but as a system

S/4HANA Asset Management, SAP Service Cloud, Predictive Asset Insights, SAP Business AI, Sustainability solutions. Each maps to a specific buyer and a specific pain. The course shows how they form a single closed loop: sensor alert → work order → dispatched technician → financial entry → updated maintenance strategy. All in one system, with no manual handoffs between departments.

You walk into conversations with real numbers, not general claims

Equinor runs condition-based maintenance on live SAP. Swiss Federal Railways applies predictive maintenance across thousands of kilometers of track. EDF saves two hours per service order by digitizing field work for wind turbine crews. These are not marketing stories — they are outcomes you can bring to a customer meeting and discuss on substance. 

Two Audiences, Two Different Conversations

The course makes a clear distinction: asset-centric and service-centric buyers are not the same people, and the conversation needs to reflect that:

  • COOs and VPs of Reliability in oil & gas, utilities, and mining think in terms of asset uptime, MTTR, and cost control

  • Service Operations leaders in equipment manufacturing or aftermarket service think in terms of SLA compliance, service revenue, and field workforce productivity

Blending these two conversations is a fast way to lose credibility in the room.

Format and Access

The course is free, available to all SAP users at learning.sap.com, self-paced, and closes with a verifiable digital badge. For presales and solution advisors working with asset-intensive industries, it's a direct investment in the quality of the customer conversation.

entry.title: Fast-Track AI Adoption in SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 with Expert Guided Implementation!
entry.content:
SAP Sales & Service Cloud Version 2 is the foundation for modern, AI-driven customer engagement, empowering teams to sell smarter and serve faster. Yet activating and adopting its embedded AI capabilities requires more than a license. Without structured guidance, organizations risk underutilizing AI across sales and service functions, limiting the return on their cloud investment.

Through the SAP Enterprise Support Advisory Council (ESAC), we are piloting a new Expert Guided Implementation (EGI) service, specifically designed to close that gap, giving customers direct access to SAP experts and a clear path from activation to adoption: “Implementation and enablement of AI Features in SAP Sales & Service Cloud Version 2”.

Participants receive exclusive early access to content and hands-on experience ahead of general availability, with the opportunity to directly shape the final offering through their feedback. This pilot is included in your cloud subscription at no additional cost, all we ask in return is your honest feedback to help us improve.

Please see this teaser video for further information.

What the Engagement Covers:

The EGI is delivered over two focused half-day sessions (approx. 2 hours each) by dedicated SAP subject matter experts. Sessions cover the AI feature landscape for Sales & Service Cloud V2, from live capability demonstrations and configuration walkthroughs to hands-on exercises tailored to your tenant. Real-time expert feedback is provided throughout, ensuring your team builds both the technical skills and the confidence to drive AI adoption independently.

What You Bring:

  • Active SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 licenses
  • Commitment to 2 half-day sessions

What You Receive:

  • AI implementation framework for Sales & Service Cloud V2
  • Configuration guidance to activate and optimize AI features
  • Hands-on exercises and enablement materials

Success Outcomes:

  • AI features activated and implemented in your tenant
  • Accelerated user adoption across sales and service lines

Ready to get started?

Contact us today to begin your AI implementation journey: esac@sap.com

entry.title: Why Your ERP Matters More in the Age of Agentic AI, Not Less
entry.content:
Agentic AI elevates the role of ERP. But only if the architecture can support what comes next.

Every enterprise running SAP has built something valuable: decades of process logic, business rules, transactional history, and institutional knowledge encoded into a system that runs the most critical operations in the company.

Now, as AI reshapes every layer of the enterprise, a question is emerging: does the ERP still matter as much, or does intelligence shift elsewhere?

The answer is unambiguous. In the age of agentic AI, AI does not merely recommend but acts. Your ERP matters more than it ever has. It holds the process context, the data semantics, and the governance infrastructure that agentic AI needs to operate reliably. Without it, AI can suggest. With it, AI can execute.

But here is the critical point: the ERP can only serve as the foundation for agentic AI if its architecture is designed for it. The investment you have made over decades is the accelerator. The question is whether the architecture enables what comes next.

ERP Is Where the Consequential Decisions Happen 

The highest-value AI in your enterprise will not draft emails or summarize meetings. It will operate where the most consequential decisions are made: financial close, supply chain optimization, procurement execution, workforce planning.

These processes live in ERP. They have always lived in ERP. And in an AI-driven enterprise, they do not move somewhere else; they become intelligent where they are.

For AI to act and execute reliably in these processes, it needs three things:

Process context. An AI agent managing your financial close must understand the full lifecycle: what triggered the process, what dependencies exist, what exceptions have occurred historically, what the downstream implications of each action are. This intelligence lives in ERP and nowhere else.

Institutional knowledge. Every enterprise has decades of business-specific decisions encoded in its systems: how exceptions are handled, which approvals are required under which conditions, what tolerances apply in which markets. Without this context, AI operates on generic assumptions, which is unacceptable for core operations.

Governance infrastructure. When AI acts autonomously within your financial reconciliation or procurement workflow, it must operate within clear boundaries: audit trails, authorization controls, compliance guardrails. This is the difference between AI the business trusts and AI the auditor rejects.

No other system holds all three. ERP is where agentic AI must live.

Core Processes Require Process-Native AI 

Consider what happens when an AI agent posts a journal entry that is off by a fraction of a cent. In isolation, trivial. Across thousands of transactions in a regulated financial close, it creates reconciliation failures, audit findings, and restatement risk. 

Or consider procurement: an AI agent that recommends a supplier based on a "best guess" rather than contractual terms, compliance status, and real-time inventory creates liability, not value. 

These are not edge cases. They represent the fundamental standard that core business processes demand: deterministic accuracy. Financial reconciliation must balance to the penny. Supply chain commitments must reflect actual constraints. Procurement decisions must stand up to audit. 

The AI built into SAP is designed for this standard. It delivers deterministic, auditable outcomes grounded in real transactional data, constrained by actual business rules, and validated against the process logic that governs your operations. It does not guess at what your data means; it knows, because it operates within the system that created and maintains that data. 

This is an architectural distinction, not a marketing one. Process-native AI understands the relationships between your transactional tables, the dependencies between process steps, and the governance rules that constrain what is permissible. That structural understanding is what makes the difference between AI that can advise and AI that can act. 

What Makes AI from SAP Different in Core Processes

The question enterprises are asking is clear: what can AI from SAP do within my core processes that other AI cannot?

It knows how your business connects. SAP's Business AI Platform encodes fifty years of enterprise process intelligence: how procure-to-pay, order-to-cash, and design-to-operate actually work at scale. When an AI agent operates within SAP, it inherits the structural understanding of how your processes interconnect. A third-party model can query your data (data is always yours). It cannot replicate this understanding of process interdependency.

Your data stays where it belongs. Enterprises with mature security postures do not expose core transactional data to external AI models. AI provided by SAP operates natively on your business data within your security boundary. There is no extraction, no external processing, no exposure of your most sensitive operational information to models you do not control.

Governance and auditability are built into the platform by design. AI Agent Hub's formal verification workflow creates an approval and ownership record for each agent, providing the audit trail needed for regulatory governance. It also logs agent sessions and captures execution traces, recording what an agent did, which tools it called, and what outcome it produced, with observability and governance capabilities being delivered in phases, intended to support audit and compliance requirements built in from the start, not added after the fact.

It reasons across the full enterprise. No single third-party AI vendor spans procure-to-pay, design-to-operate, and order-to-cash. SAP's cross-functional coverage enables AI that reasons across the enterprise, not within silos. An AI agent optimizing your supply chain understands the financial implications. An agent managing procurement sees the downstream impact on production planning.

It works with the rest of your AI landscape. AI from SAP supports the A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocol, enabling seamless coordination between SAP agents and third-party AI across your ecosystem. This is not a walled garden: it is a governed platform that interoperates with your broader technology landscape while maintaining the deterministic accuracy and audit-grade governance your core processes require.

 

Why Modernization Unlocks What Comes Next

This is where the modernization imperative becomes concrete.

Enterprises running ECC or on-premises S/4HANA have built a strong operational foundation, one that has run core business processes reliably for decades. Agentic AI introduces a new set of requirements on top of that foundation: real-time semantic interpretation, end-to-end process visibility, and governance frameworks built for autonomous action.

Many customers are already extending their environment with third-party AI tools to start capturing value now. That approach delivers real, if narrow, value. Because these tools sit outside the ERP's native process governance, they operate with less full process context, less audit-grade governance, and less reach across end-to-end process chains than natively embedded AI can provide.

On-premises ERP architecture was designed for a different era. It served that era well. Agentic AI asks something new of the architecture:

  • Real-time semantic data that AI can interpret without transformation
  • End-to-end process visibility without blind spots at system boundaries
  • Native governance frameworks designed for autonomous agents
  • A platform that evolves as AI evolves, with new capabilities on a predictable cadence

These aren't shortcomings in what you've built. They're the next requirements your investment is positioned to meet. Layering tools on top of an on-premises system extends what it can do today. Modernizing the architecture is what determines what it can become.

The Question Is Not Whether Your ERP Matters 

It matters more than ever. 

In the age of agentic AI, ERP becomes the platform where AI acts on your most consequential business decisions, with full context, full governance, and deterministic accuracy. No other system holds the process depth, institutional knowledge, and governance infrastructure that agentic AI requires. 

The question is whether your architecture enables that role. 

Your decades of SAP investment position you to capitalize on what comes next. The process knowledge is the fuel. The business understanding is the differentiator. Modernization is what transforms that investment into the AI-ready foundation your enterprise needs, where governed, process-native intelligence operates across the decisions that matter most. 

And the journey itself has changed. SAP now applies the same AI-first approach to the modernization process: an agent-led toolchain with seven purpose-built migration and modernization assistants that assess your landscape, map custom code, accelerate testing, and reduce overall effort by up to 35%. The path to an AI-ready ERP is now AI-accelerated. 

The gap widens every quarter. New AI agents are released on Cloud ERP, compounding advantage for organizations on modern foundations. The question is not whether to modernize. It is how quickly you want agentic AI working for you. 

Watch the webinar: See how the 7 migration and modernization assistants and agents accelerate your journey → https://event.on24.com/wcc/r/5365924/0A3E9159F7A3A127BFAA24CE52BE1084/6794304

entry.title: Engaging your workforce at every step with SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding
entry.content:
Organizations invest significant time and effort helping new hires gain momentum to become productive from the start, then risk losing that momentum when employees change roles, move departments, return after time away, or leave the company.

Each critical employee transition deserves an experience that helps employees feel prepared, supported, and empowered at any stage of their career. These key moments require connecting people, processes, communications, approvals, learning, and seamless coordination to ensure productivity continues through every people change.

That’s where your onboarding solution moves beyond enabling successful new hire experiences to an engine that keeps your business running smoothly through all employee transitions. With SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding, you already have the foundation to support every employee transition, unlock greater productivity, upskill talent through changes, and fuel success for both your people and your business. 

Extending the value of your SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding solution

Every employee transition is an opportunity to accelerate productivity, strengthen engagement, and deliver more consistent employee experiences. Yet, many organizations still rely on disconnected processes, manual coordination, and one-off communications to support these moments of change.

With SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding, organizations can expand the value of their existing onboarding solution to orchestrate every employee transition with consistency from one connected, intelligent solution that engages your workforce at every career milestone. The same capabilities that help new hires succeed – personalized journeys, guided tasks, learning, consistent communications, and agentic AI-driven assistance – can also support employees through internal mobility, career growth, organizational changes, offboarding, and more.

By creating consistent, connected experiences across every transition, organizations can reduce administrative effort, keep employees productive through change, and ensure every career milestone is supported with the right guidance at the right time.

Key benefits include:

  • More consistent employee experiences across every transition
  • Less manual HR and hiring manager administration
  • Faster time to productivity during changes and transitions
  • Globally scalable, standardized processes and orchestration  
  • Improved visibility and coordination
  • Reduced HR compliance cost and mitigated risk
  • Personalized journeys that support employees through every career change

Enhance experiences across the employee lifecycle 

Similar to the hiring experience, employee transitions often require addressing many of the same needs for your workforce: helping employees quickly understand their responsibilities, upskill, complete necessary activities, connect with the right people, and become successful in their next chapter. Here are a few use cases where organizations can extend their SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding solution beyond the traditional new hire experience:   

  • Internal Mobility & Role Change (Crossboarding) - When employees move into new roles, departments, or business units, they need more than a title change. A thoughtful transition helps employees understand new responsibilities, complete required training, gain system access, connect with their new team, and become productive faster – while giving managers the tools to support success from day one. Instead of treating internal moves as administrative changes in the system, organizations can turn these key moments into growth experiences that enable your talent to step into new roles with the tools, resources, and support needed to thrive.
  • Promotions & Succession (Crossboarding) - A promotion is an opportunity to set employees up for long-term success. Whether someone is stepping into a leadership position or taking on new responsibilities, they often need guidance, learning, introductions, and support as they transition into their new role. By providing a tailored crossboarding experience, employees can more easily build new skills and update their profile, gain confidence, and become productive in their new role.  
  • Organizational Changes and Legal Entity Transfers (Crossboarding) - Whether an employee is relocating, changing business units, moving internationally, joining a newly acquired organization, navigating a concurrent employment or a reorganization, transitions require coordinated experiences across multiple teams. Automated workflows and mass initiation help ensure nothing is missed and compliance requirements are met, while giving employees and your business confidence throughout the process.
  • Rehire - Returning employees are already familiar with your organization and culture, but that doesn’t mean they should skip the onboarding process. Rehires often need updated policies, refreshed training, new technology access, organizational updates, and new role-specific guidance. A tailored rehire journey recognizes their previous experience while ensuring they’re set up for success in today’s organization.
  • Offboarding - The employee experience doesn’t end with a resignation. A consistent and structured offboarding process supports compliance, coordinates equipment returns and access removal, captures key feedback, creates a knowledge transfer plan, and leaves employees with a positive final impression.   
  • Alumni - Once an employee’s time at a company ends, they may quickly feel disconnected and unsure of how to access relevant documents. Maintaining an alumni experience allows organizations to continue supporting former employees with access to post-employment resources, important documents, and communications. It also helps strengthen relationships with departing employees, making it easier to engage former employees as loyal advocates, referrals, or future rehires.

Boost continuous workforce productivity with SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding

SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding transforms onboarding processes into an autonomous workforce activation experience. AI agents seamlessly orchestrate workflows, anticipate needs, and ensure employees are connected, productive, and fully prepared for day one through every career milestone.

  • Start before day one – Use recruiting data to auto-generate tasks, communications, and workflows so employees arrive prepared and productive from the start.
  • Personalized journeys for every transition – Extend beyond onboarding to mobility, growth, and transitions that engage employees at every career milestone with guided experiences, tailored journeys, custom tasks, and configurable workflows.
  • Agent driven automation – Use intelligent Task Agents and embedded AI capabilities to automatically orchestrate work across HR, IT, and hiring managers; launching, assigning, and tracking tasks without manual follow-ups.
  • Connected data foundation, skills, and experiences – As part of the SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite, ensure trusted data, embedded compliance, real-time insights, ongoing skills development, and a continuous employee experience.
  • Global scale, local control – Deliver consistent experiences globally while dynamically meeting local regulations and compliance requirements for any employee transition.  

Every transition is an opportunity to strengthen engagement  

Every employee transition deserves a meaningful, structured, and personalized experience that supports your people and your business. By extending your SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding solution to support crossboarding, rehires, transfers, organizational changes, offboarding, and alumni, organizations can create more connected employee experiences while improving operational efficiency, coordination, and continued productivity. 

Many organizations already have the capabilities to support these journeys as part of their SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding solution – the next step is expanding how they’re used across the entire employee lifecycle. Learn more about SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding.

entry.title: Re: #CoE#CSRI Goods movement 657E and 657 in return delivey?
entry.content:
Dear @leah2,

SAP KBA 2844101 confirms that the requirement type MSR for schedule line category AR sets the special stock indicator E. The same KBA also confirms that no configuration step is currently available in Public Cloud to change this customizing. Therefore there is no standard configuration to force FI document generation for the original 657 E goods receipt while keeping Refund Control = Decide Later

If an FI document is required immediately at goods receipt the standard option is to choose an early refund option, for example Create Credit Memo Request instead of Decide Later

If the business must keep Decide Later the supported process is to complete the refund decision later in Manage Customer Returns app -> Determine Refund -> Release Selected Items. This can generate the relevant follow-up document such as a credit memo request but it does not retroactively create an FI document for the original 657 E goods receipt

Since SAP confirms that no configuration step is available for this behavior the SAP proposed path for changing/enhancing the standard behavior is to submit a feature request through SAP Customer Influence

More details below:

  • SAP KBA 2844101 – Return stock is received as a special stock
  • SAP Community Customer Returns Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud WM
  • SAP KBA 3765245 – No FI Document Generation in Returns Delivery in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • SAP KBA 3093842 – No response when trying to refund an accelerated customer return in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • SAP KBA 2963059 – How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Product

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Introducing Our New Webinar Series: Modern Identity in a Connected, High-Expectation World
entry.content:
Introducing Our New Webinar Series: Modern Identity in a Connected, High-Expectation World

Identity has quietly become one of the most important layers in the modern digital stack. It influences how customers sign up, how they log in, how they are protected, how they are understood—and ultimately, how they experience a brand.

As organizations continue to navigate rising expectations around privacy, security, and personalization, identity is no longer just a backend concern. It sits at the intersection of marketing, security, data, and customer experience.

That’s the backdrop for our upcoming webinar series focused on SAP Customer Identity and Access Management (CIAM)—a set of four focused sessions exploring how organizations are rethinking identity to drive growth, reduce risk, and simplify increasingly complex digital ecosystems.

Each session takes a deep dive into a specific challenge teams are actively working through today, from fraud and fragmentation to first-party data strategy and technical debt. And because each topic is designed to be interactive and practical, space is limited.

Below is a look at what we’ll be covering.

Session 1: Beyond Login: Turning Digital Identity into a Revenue Engine in a Cookie-Constrained World

TUESDAY, JULY 14 | 2:00 – 3:00pm EST

With third-party cookies disappearing and customer acquisition costs rising, organizations are looking for new ways to build reliable first-party data strategies without adding friction.

This session explores how identity can become a growth driver by enabling privacy-safe data collection, progressive profiling, and real-time consent management. Learn how to enrich customer profiles across touchpoints and improve attribution through identity-connected signals across your marketing and commerce ecosystem.

Register here

 

Session 2: Fraud, Friction, and the False Tradeoff: Designing Secure Authentication Without Killing Customer Experience

TUESDAY, JULY 28 | 2:00 – 3:00pm EST

Fraud is increasing—but so are expectations for seamless digital experiences. This session challenges the idea that security must come at the cost of usability.

We’ll explore how adaptive, risk-based authentication can reduce fraud while keeping trusted users on a frictionless path. Topics include passkeys, device intelligence, behavioral signals, and modern authentication patterns that balance protection with conversion.

Register here

 

Session 3: Global Customers, Local Compliance: Solving Identity Fragmentation Across Regions, Brands, and Devices

TUESDAY, AUG 11 | 2:00 – 3:00pm EST

Global organizations face growing complexity from regional regulations, brand fragmentation, and duplicate user identities across systems.

This session looks at how to unify identity across geographies while still meeting local compliance needs. Learn how to reduce fragmentation with identity linking, support data residency requirements, and enable consistent experiences across brands and channels.

Register here

 

Session 4: Identity as Infrastructure: Reducing Technical Debt While Enabling Marketing Agility

TUESDAY, AUG 25 | 2:00 – 3:00pm EST

Many organizations still treat identity as a supporting layer, but it increasingly functions as core digital infrastructure.

This session explores how a modern CIAM foundation can centralize identity services, reduce custom integrations, and enable faster innovation across marketing, commerce, and analytics. Learn how to simplify your stack while improving agility, governance, and scalability.

Register here

Join one, or join them all. We are excited to host you

entry.title: Connecting Two SAP CAP Projects Without Duplicating Your Data
entry.content:
There's a problem that comes up on almost every multi-project SAP BTP engagement, and I've seen teams solve it in some really messy ways.

You have two CAP projects. One of them — let's call it Project A — owns a set of shared constants, configuration parameters, maybe a job run log. Project B needs to read that data. So what do people do? They copy the table. Or they create an API. Or they hardcode values that really shouldn't be hardcoded.

All of these work. None of them are clean.

The proper solution is HDI cross-container synonyms. Once it's set up, Project B can query Project A's HANA tables directly — same SQL, same CAP service code, no replication, no drift between environments. It looks and feels like a local table. It just happens to live in someone else's HDI container.

I put together a Tech Bite on this recently and figured it's worth writing up properly, because the documentation is scattered and the first time you set it up you will almost certainly hit at least one of the three errors I mention at the end.

The thing nobody explains first: why it's complicated at all

HDI containers are isolated. That's the point of them — each CAP project gets its own schema, its own deploy lifecycle, its own access controls. But that isolation also means Container B can't just say "I want to read from Container A's schema" — because the schema name is dynamically assigned at deployment time. You can't hardcode it.

So SAP's solution is a User-Provided Service, or UPS. It's a simple Cloud Foundry construct — basically a key-value store with no runtime component — that carries the schema name of Project A. You register it with the -t hana tag, declare it in Project B's mta.yaml, and the HDI deployer picks it up during deployment and injects the correct schema wherever you've referenced it.

That's it. That's the whole trick. Everything else — the synonym files, the config files, the grants — is just plumbing on top of that core idea.

What you're actually building in Project B

Once you understand what the UPS is doing, the rest makes sense. You need four types of files:

.hdiconfig tells the deployer which plugins to use. You need two of these — one in db/src/ and one in db/cfg/. It's a small JSON file, easy to get wrong by forgetting one of the two locations.

.hdbsynonym is where you define the local alias. So instead of referencing PROJECT_A_SCHEMA.CONSTANTS_TABLE (which you can't hardcode anyway), you just write CONSTANTS_TABLE and map it to the actual technical object name in Project A. This lives in db/src/.

.hdbsynonymconfig is the file that connects the synonym to the UPS. This is what makes the schema resolution dynamic. It lives in db/cfg/ — deliberately separate from db/src/ to avoid deployment conflicts. The key thing here is that the value you put in schema.configure must start with exactly the same string you used as the key in mta.yaml. More on this when I get to the errors.

.hdbgrants is the access control piece. Project B's container needs to be explicitly granted a role from Project A. That role needs to already exist over in Project A — it's not something you create in Project B. Your Project A team needs to have a role set up that grants SELECT on the tables you need.

The setup, briefly

On the Cloud Foundry side, you create the UPS once:

cf create-user-provided-service ProjectA_UPS \
  -p '{"schema": "YOUR_PROJECT_A_SCHEMA"}' -t hana

Then in Project B's mta.yaml, you declare it as an existing service resource and bind it to the db-deployer module under SERVICE_REPLACEMENTS:

- name: ProjectA-UPS-resource
  group: SERVICE_REPLACEMENTS
  properties:
    key: ProjectA_UPS
    service: ~{service-name}

Your synonym config then references it like this:

"schema.configure": "ProjectA_UPS/schema"

And once everything deploys, your CAP service code doesn't need to know any of this happened. You just query it:

const db = await cds.connect.to("db");
const results = await db.run(
  `SELECT * FROM "CONSTANTS_TABLE" WHERE "PARAM" = ?`, [value]
);

Clean. No different from querying a local table.

The three errors you will probably hit

I'm not going to pretend this always works first time. Here's what goes wrong most often:

"synonym config refers to unknown service replacement" — This one is almost always a key mismatch. The string you put as key in mta.yaml and the prefix before /schema in your .hdbsynonymconfig need to be identical. Character for character. Check both files side by side.

"insufficient privilege" — The role you referenced in .hdbgrants doesn't exist in Project A, or it exists but doesn't include SELECT on the tables you're trying to reach. You'll need to go back to whoever owns Project A and check what roles are actually deployed there. HANA DB Explorer is your friend here.

"object not found" — The object name in your .hdbsynonym is wrong. The technical name of a table in HDI usually follows a namespace pattern with underscores replacing dots — something like HR_HTR_UTILITY_OBJECTS_CONSTANTS_TABLE. The only reliable way to find the exact name is to look it up directly in HANA DB Explorer under Project A's schema.

One thing worth knowing for local development

When you run cds watch locally, you're using SQLite — there's no HDI container, no synonym. So you need a workaround for local testing.

The simplest approach is to create a CDS entity with @CDS.persistence.exists that mirrors the external table's structure, and seed it with a CSV file. It won't be connected to Project A's live data, but it lets you develop and test the rest of your logic without needing a full BTP deployment every time.

Worth the setup

The first time you wire this up it feels like a lot of moving parts. And honestly, it is. But once it's done, it just works — and it works cleanly. No sync jobs, no API calls for what is essentially a config lookup, no risk of the two sides drifting apart over time.

If your project has shared reference data living in another team's HDI container, this is the right way to access it.

Happy to answer questions in the comments if you hit something I haven't covered here.

#SAP #SAPCAP #SAPHANA #BTP #SAPDevelopment #HDI #CloudFoundry #PwC

entry.title: From Gross Revenue to Gross Margin: A Practitioner's Guide to Margin Management
entry.content:
Margin is often explained with a simple formula: margin = revenue − cost. The reality is more nuanced, especially when you sell across multiple products, channels and regions. That’s why many organizations can see revenue quickly but still struggle to explain profitability until after the period ends.

Think of margin management as a journey: Gross Revenue → Net Revenue → Gross Margin. Each step introduces a different kind of complexity, and in SAP terms, we leverage different capabilities.

Managing the journey from revenue to margin

Figure 1 - Managing the journey from revenue to margin

Note: For simplicity, in this blog we are not considering intercompany or tax complexity

 

The three margin levels every business should distinguish

 

1) Gross Revenue

Gross revenue begins with the sales price (or invoice price). It’s typically visible immediately. But it’s rarely the true picture, because commercial programs and post-sale adjustments can change the realized revenue significantly.

2) Net Revenue

Net revenue is gross revenue after commercial conditions are applied. These include  promotions and discounts, customer rebates, billbacks, and market development funds (MDF).

This is where surprises often appear, especially with complex B2B agreements. Many of these adjustments are off‑invoice, conditional on volume or performance, and settled later. If they are not modeled and accrued accurately, net revenue is overstated and the margin looks better than it really is.

3) Gross Margin

From net revenue, the focus shifts to costs. Direct costs (COGS) are usually linkable to products. Indirect costs such as stores, contact centers, warehousing, logistics, shared services, and overhead are not.

This is where cost‑to‑serve and activity‑based costing (ABC) come into play. Indirect costs need to be allocated using operational drivers (shipments, service interactions, store footprint, etc.).

A quick example in consumer goods.

Imagine a company selling a smartphone through retail stores and direct online. Direct costs (the handset BOM, procurement, and warranty provisioning) are consistent per unit. But indirect costs behave very differently:

  • Store channel includes rent, staff, utilities and in‑store marketing. These costs are best allocated by store traffic, transactions, floor area, or staffed hours.
  • Online includes e‑commerce platform cost, payment fees, fulfillment, shipping, and returns handling—costs best allocated by orders, parcels, return rates, and handling time.

Without allocation logic, the same phone can appear to have the “same margin” across channels when the cost-to-serve is actually very different.

 

Selecting the right tool for each margin lever

SAP’s margin management portfolio maps naturally to the journey from gross revenue to net revenue, and to gross margin:

  • Margin Analysis in SAP S/4HANA provides the real-time profitability foundation for actuals with a consistent view across product, customer, channel, and region. Universal Allocation standardizes allocations and top-down distributions, so costs are aligned across these dimensions in Margin Analysis.
  • SAP Margin Optimization solutions by Vistex support the gross‑to‑net portion. This models complex rebates and incentives (including billbacks/MDF and other off‑invoice conditions) and can simulate the net margin impact of commercial agreements before they are signed-off.
  • SAP Profitability and Performance Management (PaPM) extends SAP S/4HANA indirect costing with advanced modeling for cost allocation, cost‑to‑serve, and multi-step simulations that often require additional data and allocation logic beyond standard ERP processing.

Together, these capabilities support a simple operating model:

 

Capability

What it answers

Example outcome

Margin Analysis in SAP S/4HANA

What has happened to my margins?Real-time visibility into revenue, cost, and margin.

SAP Profitability and Performance Management (PaPM)

Why are my margins changing?Insight into cost drivers, allocations, and profitability scenarios.

SAP Margin Optimization solutions by Vistex

How can I improve my gross-to-net?Optimized pricing, rebates, and incentive programs.

Table 1- Complementary capabilities

 

Actuals, forecasts, and simulation: making margin actionable

Margin management also has a timing dimension. SAP S/4HANA Margin Analysis supports profitability insights during the period. Indirect costs can be allocated on a cadence that matches the business, supporting a continuous-closing mindset rather than waiting for period-end.

From there, teams look ahead: short-term rolling forecasts often require granular detail (product/customer/channel), while long-term plans are more aggregated (country/category/line of business). Margin simulation sits between these worlds: the what‑if bridge that helps teams respond quickly to change, making decisions that will keep margins tracking to forecasts.

Returning to the mobile phone example:
If transportation costs rise (due to an oil price spike or route disruption), the company needs to understand the margin impact by channel and customer agreement. Store replenishment costs may increase due to frequent shipments, while online margins may be hit harder by last‑mile delivery and returns. Modeling of both cost-to-serve and gross-to-net helps answer practical questions such as:

  • Should we introduce a shipping surcharge or raise prices selectively?
  • Should we adjust promotional funding, rebates, or MDF levels for certain partners?
  • Should we change service levels (e.g., free shipping thresholds) or reroute fulfillment to reduce cost?

These operational decisions connect commercial levers and cost levers using consistent data in SAP S/4HANA shared by each of the capabilities.

Use cases, products and primary users

Figure 2- Use cases, products and primary users

 

Conclusion

Margin is a journey from gross revenue, through gross‑to‑net adjustments, to gross margin after direct and indirect costs. SAP supports this end‑to‑end journey with a coherent set of capabilities: Margin Analysis for real-time, reconciled actuals; PaPM for advanced allocations and simulation; and Margin Optimization to manage the commercial programs that impact the gross-to-net.

 

Authors

John Fallon – Global Product Marketing Manager for Pricing, SAP

Michel Haesendonckx -  VP, Global Functional Lead – oCFO, SAP

 

Appendix – Products Referenced

SAP Margin Analysis

  • Margin Analysis is a capability in SAP S/4HANA for profitability analysis based on the Universal Journal.
  • It combines financial and management accounting data in one place and supports near real-time insight.
  • Users can monitor margin by dimensions like product, customer, or channel as transactions occur.
  • It supports continuous closing by allowing frequent allocations and updates.

Learn more

 

SAP Profitability and Performance Management (PaPM)

  • PaPM extends SAP S/4HANA with advanced profitability modeling and cost allocation.
  • It supports activity-based costing, multi-step allocations, and scenario modeling.
  • Models can use operational drivers and supplementary data not maintained in ERP.
  • It is useful for explaining margin changes and allocating indirect costs (for example, cost-to-serve).

Product page

 

SAP Margin Optimization solutions by Vistex

  • These solutions manage complex pricing and incentive agreements such as rebates, billbacks, and MDF.
  • Supports simulation of expected net revenue outcomes before agreements are signed.
  • The aim is to align commercial programs with finance’s view of net revenue and reduce surprises in gross-to-net.
  • Applicable where gross-to-net complexity, accrual accuracy, or rebate settlement are known pain points.

Overview page

entry.title: TimeClock Plus integration with EC Payroll?
entry.content:
Hello team, does anyone have experience with integrating EC Payroll with TimeClock Plus (TCP)? This solution came recommended from our IT group and their website mentions integration with SAP. But I can't find any references to it in the SAP community.

Thank you!
